map.title: Iron & Steel Value Chain
map.desc: From iron ore mining through ironmaking, steelmaking, rolling and finishing to market. Uganda holds 350 million tonnes of high-grade hematite ore (60–65% Fe) — but operates almost entirely in downstream rolling.

phases.name: Phase V
phases.label:
Rolling &
Finishing
phases.stat: ~70% of installed capacity; rebar net exporter

map.gap: CRITICAL GAP: Flat steel entirely absent. USD 219.5m imports annually (HS 7208). No hot-rolled coil capacity in Uganda.

constraints.label: Upstream feedstock gap — no commercial ironmaking from domestic ore
Electricity reliability & cost (8–10¢/kWh vs Kenya 5–7¢)
Import competition — flat steel (USD 219.5m) wholly imported
Capital cost (18–23% interest vs Kenya 13–17%)
Tax policy inconsistency (CPC letters, VAT on coal)
Logistics (USD 0.08–0.12/tonne-km; no rail to ore)
Skills gap (~68% firms struggle to find technical skills)
Standards enforcement (UNBS lab not yet equipped)

priorities: Sponge iron / DRI anchored on domestic ore — missing midstream; unlocks the entire chain
Flat steel production — largest import line USD 219.5m; NO domestic production; biggest import-substitution prize
Long products (rebar/sections) — already competitive, exporting ~USD 55.8m; defend and scale
Iron ore mining & beneficiation — enabling upstream step; 1,000,000 tpa commercial mine needed

map.title: Copper & Allied Metals Value Chain
map.desc: From copper ore mining at Kilembe through smelting, refining, and semi-fabrication to insulated wire & cable. Kilembe (dormant since 1980s) holds >6.5m tonnes of copper ore. A production-sharing agreement was signed March 2025.

phases.name: Phase I
phases.label:
Mining &
Concentration
phases.stat: Kilembe dormant since 1980s; PSA signed Mar 2025

phases.name: Phase IV
phases.label: Semi-fabrication
phases.stat: Wire rod imported; largely from China

map.gap: CRITICAL GAP: Phases I–IV entirely absent. USD 57.7m cable imports (HS 8544) + USD 6.9m cathode imports. Raw ore potential untapped since 1980s.

current.label: Mine Status
current.value: Dormant since 1980s; PSA signed 3 March 2025 (Sarrai Group + Nile Fibreboard)

companies: Cable Corporation Ltd (Mehta Group — est. 1968; building wire, power cables, conductors)
Sarrai Group + Nile Fibreboard Ltd (Kilembe PSA investors — signed 3 Mar 2025)

constraints.label: Dormant midstream — Kilembe idle since 1980s; all cathode/rod imported
Capital & long lead time — mine redevelopment multi-year and capital-intensive
Energy reliability — electro-refining is power-intensive and continuous
Logistics / Kasese access — landlocked 18–20% of sale price; Kilembe upgrades needed
Historic tailings & environmental liability — acid-mine-drainage risk
Standards & substandard cable imports — safety-critical enforcement gap
Skills — mining/metallurgical skills scarce after decades of dormancy

priorities: Insulated wire & cable — expand Cable Corporation-led industry; capture USD 57.7m import market + electrification-driven growth
Kilembe copper cathode & cobalt — execute 2025 PSA; domestic cable feedstock + cobalt as battery-mineral export
Copper wire rod semi-fabrication — integrate midstream between cathode and cable domestically

rows.label: Cobalt positioning
rows.target: High-value battery-mineral export alongside copper cathode

map.title: Automotive Value Chain
map.desc: From raw materials (steel, copper, plastics) through component manufacture, vehicle assembly, and distribution to the after-market. Kiira Motors Corporation (KMC) plant commissioned 27 September 2025 in Jinja.

phases.name: Phase II
phases.label: Components
phases.detail: Body panels, seats, wiring, batteries, tyres

map.gap: KEY GAP: Components (Phase II) — Uganda assembles but does not manufacture components. Local content almost zero. USD 31.7m parts imports alone (HS 8708).

current.label: KMC Plant
current.value: Kiira Vehicle Plant, Jinja — commissioned 27 Sept 2025

current.label: KMC Steel Demand
current.value: ~37,300 tonnes of steel for bus frames by 2027/28 (~USD 75m)

current.label: Financing Gap
current.value: ~UGX 50 billion gap for KMC FY2025/26 scale-up

companies: Kiira Motors Corporation (KMC) — Jinja; state-anchored; Kayoola EV buses; plant commissioned Sept 2025
Bajaj / TVS assemblers — CKD/SKD motorcycle assembly (boda-boda market)
Bus/truck body fabricators — Kampala/Jinja; bodies on imported chassis
After-market parts cluster — Ndeeba/Kisekka; large import-based spare parts trade

constraints.label: Finance/capital for KMC scale-up (~UGX 50bn FY2025/26 gap)
Shallow local content — CKD/SKD kit dependence; domestic components almost zero
Used-import competition — no age-limit or taxation policy to direct demand to domestic assembly
Technology & tooling — assembly established but component manufacture absent
Skills — automotive/mechatronics and EV skills scarce
Logistics — landlocked raises CKD-kit costs (~18–20% of sale price)
Standards & homologation — vehicle standards and age limits shape market

priorities: Buses and electric buses (KMC anchor) — leverage new plant, EV differentiator, government fleet procurement
Motorcycle assembly (CKD → e-boda) — largest-volume mobility market (USD 157m imports); feasible, job-rich
Spare parts & component localisation — builds local content; supplies assembly + large after-market; deepens steel/copper/plastics links

rows.label: Local content
rows.target: Domestic steel, copper, plastics inputs significantly localised

rows.label: Vehicle import bill
rows.target: Significantly reduced via domestic assembly + used-import policy

map.title: Textiles & Garments Value Chain
map.desc: From cotton growing through ginning, spinning, weaving, finishing and garment manufacture to market. Uganda has 39 ginneries with ~1 million bale annual capacity — running at only ~10%.

phases.name: Phase V
phases.label:
Wet Processing
& Finishing
phases.stat: CRITICAL MISSING STEP — blocks whole chain

map.gap: CRITICAL GAP: Wet processing & finishing (Phase V) — without dyeing and finishing, Uganda cannot produce export-quality fabric or garments. This single gap blocks the entire chain.

current.label: Cotton Output
current.value: ~116,000 bales (2023); up to 250,000 in good years

current.label: Ginneries
current.value: 39 ginneries; ~1 million bale annual capacity at ~10% use

companies: Southern Range Nyanza (Nytil) — one of two integrated mills
Fine Spinners Ltd — integrated; ~USD 50m turnover; ~18,000 employment incl. outgrowers
Lira Spinning Facility — new investment under cotton-development programme

constraints.label: Wet-processing/finishing gap — dyeing/finishing absent; blocks garment scale-up
Used-clothing (mivumba) competition — directly undercuts domestic garments
Cotton output volatility & low yields — only 10% of ginnery capacity used
Capital for spinning/weaving — long-term finance scarce and costly
Water & effluent for wet-processing — dyeing needs treatable water; NEMA compliance costly
Skills — textile-engineering and supervisory skills limited
Logistics — landlocked costs vs Asian exporters

priorities: Garments & apparel (incl. institutional uniforms) — most job-rich step; anchor with institutional demand + EAC market
Yarn & fabric (spinning/weaving + wet-processing/finishing) — process domestic lint instead of exporting raw; add missing finishing
Cottonseed oil & cake — capture ginning by-products (edible oil, animal feed); feasible, green, quick value-add

rows.label: Garment employment
rows.target: One of Uganda's largest manufacturing employers

map.title: Pharmaceuticals Value Chain
map.desc: From API and excipient supply through formulation, packaging, distribution to dispensing. Uganda currently imports ~90% of medicines despite having 6 WHO-GMP aligned manufacturers led by QCIL.

phases.name: Phase I
phases.label:
API &
Excipients
phases.stat: Near-total import dependence; API all imported

map.gap: KEY GAP: API & excipients (Phase I) — near-total import dependence is the deepest structural and supply-security risk. India (58% of medicine imports) and China dominate.

companies: Cipla Quality Chemical Industries Ltd (QCIL) — WHO-GMP prequalified; ARVs, antimalarials, NCDs; UGX 267bn revenue

constraints.label: API/excipient import dependence — deepest structural and supply-security risk
WHO-GMP / quality-systems depth — capability concentrated at QCIL
Capital + long regulatory lead times — GMP plants capital-intensive; multi-year approval cycles
Procurement/market access — NMS/JMS preferential procurement and NDA fees shape viability
Skills — pharmacists, QA/QC, regulatory-affairs specialists in short supply
Reliable power & pharma-grade utilities — GMP needs uninterrupted clean power and water
Cold-chain & distribution logistics

priorities: Essential generics (ARVs, antimalarials, anti-TB, priority NCD) — build on QCIL's WHO-GMP base; guaranteed domestic + donor/regional demand
Medical consumables and basic formulations — feasible, job-creating, broad import substitution
Selective local API/excipient production — strategic longer-term to reduce deepest dependency

rows.label: Import dependence
rows.target: Reduced to <50% via expanded domestic manufacturing

rows.label: API production
rows.target: Selective domestic API for highest-volume products

map.title: Petrochemicals & Fertilizers Value Chain
map.desc: Two parallel sub-chains: petroleum refining → petrochemicals, and phosphate mining → fertilisers. The Kabaale refinery (60,000 bpd, ~USD 4bn) is expected operational ~2029. The Sukulu phosphate plant (Tororo) is stalled.

phases.name: Phase I
phases.label:
Upstream
Extraction
phases.stat: Oil production pre-FID; Sukulu phosphate stalled

phases.name: Phase IV
phases.label:
Blending &
Formulation
phases.detail: NPK blends, LPG filling, plastics conversion

map.gap: CRITICAL GAPS: Refinery not operational until ~2029. Sukulu phosphate stalled. Uganda imports ~50,000 tonnes of fertiliser vs ~1 million tonnes needed — a 950,000 tonne annual gap.

current.label: Kabaale Refinery
current.value: 60,000 bpd; ~USD 4bn; Alpha MBM + UNOC (~40%); expected ops ~2029–30

current.label: Sukulu Phosphate Plant
current.value: 100,000 tpa design; Tororo; shut 2021; briefly resumed 2023; halted again

current.label: Fertiliser Gap
current.value: ~50,000 tonnes supplied vs ~1,000,000 tonnes needed — 95% gap

current.label: Kabalega Petrochemical Park
current.value: Future; post-refinery; polymer feedstock for plastics chain

companies: Alpha MBM Investments (UAE) + UNOC — Kabaale refinery (PSA signed March 2025)
Uganda National Oil Company (UNOC) — ~40% refinery stake

constraints.label: Stalled phosphate processing — Sukulu shut; ~50k t vs ~1m t need
Very large capital + financing fragility — refinery USD 4bn; Sukulu collapse shows fragility
Feedstock timing — refinery pre-2029; no domestic petrochemical feedstock until ~2029–30
Fertiliser quality & distribution — adulterated fertiliser (UNBS); rural distribution weak
Environmental management — refinery emissions; phosphogypsum hazard
Skills — chemical/process engineering scarce

priorities: Fertiliser blending + revival of phosphate processing (Sukulu or green ammonia alternative)
Sulphuric acid and basic industrial chemicals — paired with phosphate processing
Petrochemicals/polymers at Kabalega — transformational medium-term; sequenced to refinery ~2029–30

rows.label: Refinery status
rows.target: Operational; supplying domestic fuel + polymer feedstock

rows.label: Petrochemical park
rows.target: Operational; supplying domestic resin to plastics chain

map.title: Sugar & Confectionery Value Chain
map.desc: From sugarcane growing through milling, refining, and downstream diversification (confectionery, ethanol, co-generation). Uganda produces ~822,000 tonnes/year with a structural surplus of 180,000+ tonnes.

phases.name: Phase V
phases.label: Market
phases.stat: Exporter; but USD 38.1m industrial sugar imported

map.gap: KEY GAP: Industrial sugar refining (Phase III) — USD 38.1m imported despite domestic surplus. Downstream (Phase IV) — ethanol from molasses and co-gen from bagasse largely unexploited.

current.label: Kakira Output Share
current.value: ~50% of national output; >2 million tonnes cane crushed/year

companies: Kakira Sugar Works — ~50% of national output; crushing >2m tonnes cane/yr
Kinyara Sugar — industrial-sugar refinery expanded to ~75,000 t (2023)

constraints.label: Over-supply / over-licensing of raw sugar — 180,000+ t surplus; continued mill licensing strains cane per mill
Downstream capital & technology — refining, ethanol distilleries, confectionery need investment
Industrial sugar quality/standards — food/pharma buyers require industrial-grade specs (UNBS)
Grid feed-in for co-generation — bagasse power needs clear feed-in/tariff framework
Distillery effluent — vinasse and bagasse ash management
Out-grower coordination & cane quality

priorities: Industrial/refined sugar — substitute USD 38.1m import line using surplus domestic cane; serves beverages, food processing, pharmaceuticals
Ethanol (fuel & industrial) — convert molasses surplus into fuel-blending ethanol; strong regional demand
Co-generation (bagasse to grid) — monetise bagasse as renewable grid electricity; improve mill economics

rows.label: Chain value
rows.target: Downstream-dominated: industrial sugar, ethanol, co-gen, confectionery

rows.label: Industrial sugar
rows.target: Domestic refining meets full industrial demand

map.title: Plastics & Packaging Value Chain
map.desc: From resin/feedstock through conversion, packaging production, use and recycling. All virgin resin is imported. ~600 tonnes of plastic waste generated daily; <40% properly collected.

phases.name: Phase II
phases.label: Conversion
phases.stat: Established; Nice House, Nile Plastic, others

map.gap: KEY GAP: Resin feedstock (Phase I) — 100% imported. Recycling (Phase V) — severely under-scaled despite 600t/day waste. Domestic resin awaits Kabalega petrochemical park (~2029).

current.label: Policy
current.value: National Plastics Circularity Strategy 2023–2028; 2025 single-use-bag ban (NEMA)

companies: Nice House of Plastics — largest converter; est. 1970; Kampala area

constraints.label: 100% imported virgin resin — no domestic resin until petrochemical park
Low waste collection / weak circularity — ~600 t/day waste; <40% collected
Capital & fragmentation — recycling plants and modern converters need capital
Policy transition risk — single-use-ban opportunity for recyclers but risk for single-use producers
Standards & substandard bags — UNBS certification on thickness and food-grade
Logistics — imported resin bears landlocked penalty

priorities: Industrial and food/beverage packaging — substitute USD 37m+ imports with standards-compliant domestic packaging
Plastics recycling (PET/HDPE flake & pellet) — scale to turn 600 t/day waste into domestic feedstock; aligns with 2025 circularity policy
Agricultural and cement packaging (woven sacks, films) — feasible, job-rich, linked to agriculture and cement sectors

rows.label: Packaging demand
rows.target: Several-fold increase as all manufacturing sectors scale

rows.label: Domestic resin
rows.target: Kabalega park operational; domestic polymer feedstock

rows.label: Recycling
rows.target: Circularity Strategy target: ~37% plastic pollution cut by 2040

rows.label: Supply chain
rows.target: Domestic conversion + recycled content + domestic resin

map.title: Cement & Building Materials Value Chain
map.desc: From limestone quarrying through clinker production, cement grinding, and downstream building materials to construction market. Uganda grinds well but imports >50% of its clinker. Two new clinker plants (totalling ~USD 500m) are under construction.

phases.name: Phase II
phases.label:
Clinker
Production
phases.stat: >50% clinker imported; 2 new plants under construction

map.gap: KEY GAP: Clinker (Phase II) — >50% imported; USD 162.2m import bill (2024). Closing this gap is the sector's #1 priority. Two new plants under construction are the right response.

current.label: Cement Exports
current.value: USD 86.0m to Rwanda, DRC, South Sudan, Kenya

current.label: New Clinker Investment
current.value: NE Uganda: USD 300m, >6,000 t/day + Buikwe: USD 200m, ~4,000 t/day (2025)

companies: Hima Cement — ~2.0 Mtpa; owns Dura limestone quarry (Kamwenge)
NE Uganda clinker plant — USD 300m new investment; >6,000 t/day (under construction)
West International Holding — USD 200m Buikwe clinker plant ~4,000 t/day (2025)

constraints.label: Clinker capacity gap — >50% imported despite strong grinding; USD 162.2m import line
Energy cost & reliability — clinker kilns highly energy-intensive; decisive for competitiveness
Capital intensity — clinker kilns USD 200–300m; large but appetite shown
Limestone licensing & land — reserves exist but licensing must keep pace with demand
Logistics — bulk/low-density; landlocked penalty protects domestic market but raises input costs
Kiln emissions/dust — carbon- and dust-intensive; NEMA-regulated; alt-fuel opportunity
Standards — substandard cement a safety/market issue; UNBS enforcement critical

priorities: Clinker production — close the >50% import gap; support USD 200–300m new plants; alt. fuels + SCM substitution (steel slag, phosphogypsum, pozzolana)
Finished cement for regional export — defend and grow USD 86m export position, strengthened by domestic clinker
Higher-value building materials — fibre-cement, tiles, precast/concrete products

rows.label: Clinker self-sufficiency
rows.target: >90% — two new plants under construction are the start

rows.label: Regional export
rows.target: Uganda as dominant regional cement supplier

rows.label: Circular materials
rows.target: Steel slag + phosphogypsum → SCM; waste → alt. kiln fuel
